raw_output_payload.content:
```bash
while IFS= read -r f; do stat -c %s "$f" 2>/dev/null; done < /workspace/files.txt | awk '{s+=$1} END{print s}'
```
raw_output_payload.reasoning: We need to read file paths from /workspace/files.txt, sum their sizes in bytes. Use du -b? But du gives block size. Use stat -c %s. So: awk to read lines, then sum. Command: awk '{sum+=($1?system("stat -c %s \""$1"\" 2>/dev/null"):0)} END{print sum}' /workspace/files.txt. But system inside awk not good. Better: while read f; do stat -c %s "$f" 2>/dev/null; done < file | awk '{s+=$1} END{print s}'. So single command: awk? Let's produce: awk '{print $1}'? Actually simpler: while read -r f; do stat -c %s "$f" 2>/dev/null; done < /workspace/files.txt | awk '{s+=$1} END{print s}'. Provide as one line.

So: awk to read lines, then sum. Command: awk '{sum+=($1?system("stat -c %s \""$1"\" 2>/dev/null"):0)} END{print sum}' /workspace/files.txt. But system inside awk not good. Better: while read f; do stat -c %s "$f" 2>/dev/null; done < file | awk '{s+=$1} END{print s}'. So single command: awk? Let's produce: awk '{print $1}'? Actually simpler: while read -r f; do stat -c %s "$f" 2>/dev/null; done < /workspace/files.txt | awk '{s+=$1} END{print s}'. Provide as one line.
